## [00:00 - 03:05]

Hey, so take a look at this video here where I am showing you offline conversions versus website purchases. So website purchases are based on the pixels being the browser pixel and cappy, which is the server pixel offline conversions are uploaded directly from your sales tool, your back end. In this case, it's Shopify. So this is an e-commerce ad account. I want to illustrate to you something that I've known for a long time, but I guess a lot of people haven't realized or don't understand or don't realize is that when you upload offline conversions, what you're doing is you're pushing data that Facebook has not captured through the pixel back into Facebook. Benefit number one is that anything that hasn't been tracked as a conversion can now be tracked. And number two, you can use the offline conversion data for custom audiences for lookalikes and for optimization as well. That's a whole different ballgame. What I want to show you right now is the illustration of the impact it could have. Now, the first part of this is you might be asking, why can't Facebook see this data? Number one, iOS 14. So it's the conversion comes through and someone's opted out of iOS 14 tracking. It could slip through. But secondly, and this one's quite important because it would probably apply to maybe half of e-commerce retailers and online stores, is the website Pixel will only track conversions that have happened in the last seven days because the optimization period has changed. If a conversion happens eight days after the click, Facebook's Pixel can't kind of see it. However, right now and at this moment in time, the offline conversions that you upload, and there'll be a link below in this or one of the other guides, when you upload that offline conversion data, Facebook can match up to 28 days after the click. So for sale does happen in this case, in this particular e-commerce stores case, we know based on pre iOS 14 data that sales often double the day the click was made two weeks later. So for example, if we know that ROAS is one X today, we know by two weeks time, we two X or if it's two X today, it'll be four X because there's a consideration path that people are going down. It's $80 AOV, sometimes $100 and people are browsing, they're shopping at different for different ideas. So because of that pre iOS 14, we could model one day click data, seven day click data and 28 day click data. Just to look at how people work converting on Facebook, we can look at Google Analytics, we can see the last click data in this case, mostly it's email and some brand search. But now by uploading offline conversions in the new world that we're in, we can actually see the data as well as the breakdowns.

## [06:11 - 09:13]

There are some Android conversions and these are more than likely conversions have happened outside of the tracking period as well as these ones here. But the majority are iPhone. Can we use it to validate our optimizations? And my view on this is yes, you can. ## [09:13 - 09:55]

But at least I can know that it's actually generating better revenues than otherwise would be reported by the pixel. So, you know, the pixel is the least accurate of your reporting.
